text:
The year 2020 was a year of science fiction, not in the sense of providing an escape, but in the sense of facing it head-on. Although the genre tends to bring to mind the image of far-off galaxies and advanced technology, its roots are deeply rooted in the real human issues. People who write science fiction and fantasy are struggling with the world as they are, and their fiction is bound to show social anxieties, systemic failures, and visions of possible futures. Speculative fiction proved to be a valuable tool in comprehending challenging periods throughout the year, not just as entertainment, but as a conceptual tool to envision change.

The strongest publications of the year were not based on moralizing. Rather, they delved into world and social problems with immersive storytelling and character-driven narratives. These novels explored oppression, economic inequality, and racism by having heroes who struggled to survive, defend their families, or redefine shattered systems. Together, these narratives serve as road maps to hope, implying gradual steps to a more just world by imagining and empathizing.

Shorefall by Robert Jackson Bennett is one such example. The novel is a continuation of the world introduced in Foundryside, where magic is programmable logic, performed by a practice called scriving. Artificial intent can be given to objects, and reality can be manipulated. Sancia Grado, who was made more sensitive to this unseen architecture of the universe in the original novel, aided in the saving of Tevanne with an unlikely alliance of allies.

Bennett adds to this universe in Shorefall by bringing in an ancient threat. Crasedes, a character who once made himself a god by scriving, is brought back to life after centuries of failure. He does not want chaos as an end in itself, but the radical rebuilding of humanity in the quest to achieve what he sees as better. The narrative of the perils of unchecked authority and ideological confidence is examined as Sancia and her friends seek to halt him. Under the action is a subtle analysis of inequality and how structural imbalance can divide societies and lead to revolutionary consequences.
